abstract: Online reinforcement learning (RL) has been central to post-training language models, but its extension to diffusion models remains challenging due to intractable likelihoods. Recent works discretize the reverse sampling process to enable GRPO-style training, yet they inherit fundamental drawbacks, including solver restrictions, forward-reverse inconsistency, and complicated integration with classifier-free guidance (CFG). We introduce Diffusion Negative-aware FineTuning (DiffusionNFT), a new online RL paradigm that optimizes diffusion models directly on the forward process via flow matching. DiffusionNFT contrasts positive and negative generations to define an implicit policy improvement direction, naturally incorporating reinforcement signals into the supervised learning objective. This formulation enables training with arbitrary black-box solvers, eliminates the need for likelihood estimation, and requires only clean images rather than sampling trajectories for policy optimization. DiffusionNFT is up to $25\times$ more efficient than FlowGRPO in head-to-head comparisons, while being CFG-free. For instance, DiffusionNFT improves the GenEval score from 0.24 to 0.98 within 1k steps, while FlowGRPO achieves 0.95 with over 5k steps and additional CFG employment. By leveraging multiple reward models, DiffusionNFT significantly boosts the performance of SD3.5-Medium in every benchmark tested.

keyInsights: Optimizing the forward diffusion process, rather than the reverse, offers a principled way to apply RL to diffusion models, bypassing issues of likelihood intractability and maintaining consistency.

Reinforcement signals can be effectively integrated into a supervised learning objective by implicitly defining and contrasting 'positive' and 'negative' velocity predictors based on reward-transformed data.
Decoupling the sampling and training policies with soft updates and utilizing advanced ODE samplers for data collection significantly enhances stability and efficiency in online diffusion RL.
originalProblem: Applying online reinforcement learning to diffusion models is challenging due to intractable likelihoods required by conventional policy gradient algorithms.
Existing methods that discretize the reverse (denoising) process for RL lead to 'forward inconsistency,' solver restrictions, and complex integration of Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG).
Computational inefficiency and memory-intensive data collection limit the scalability and practical application of current diffusion RL techniques.
results: DiffusionNFT demonstrates 3x to 25x greater efficiency and superior performance over FlowGRPO, achieving a GenEval score of 0.98 within 1,000 training steps (approx. 100 GPU hours) while being CFG-free.

When jointly optimized on multiple reward models, the CFG-free DiffusionNFT model outperforms larger, CFG-based models like SD3.5-L (8B parameters) and FLUX.1-Dev (12B parameters) on various benchmarks (e.g., GenEval, PickScore, HPSv2.1).

Ablation studies confirm the necessity of the negative loss component to prevent online training collapse, the benefits of ODE samplers for data quality, and the importance of adaptive loss weighting and soft policy updates for stable and fast convergence.
solution: DiffusionNFT performs online reinforcement learning directly on the forward diffusion process, optimizing the model's velocity predictor through a flow matching objective.

It transforms raw rewards into 'optimality probabilities' to implicitly categorize data into positive and negative samples, guiding policy improvement through a 'Negative-aware FineTuning' (NFT) loss.

The approach decouples sampling and training, allowing the use of arbitrary black-box solvers for data collection, requires only clean images for optimization, and inherently operates without Classifier-Free Guidance.
paper_summary.summary: DiffusionNFT introduces an online reinforcement learning framework for diffusion models, optimizing the forward (noising) process via a flow matching objective and negative-aware fine-tuning. This method achieves 3x to 25x greater efficiency and superior performance compared to existing approaches, notably generating high-quality images without Classifier-Free Guidance and outperforming larger, CFG-based models in multi-reward settings.

title: DiffusionNFT: Online Diffusion Reinforcement with Forward Process

abstract: We propose QeRL, a Quantization-enhanced Reinforcement Learning framework for large language models (LLMs). While RL is essential for LLMs' reasoning capabilities, it is resource-intensive, requiring substantial GPU memory and long rollout durations. QeRL addresses these issues by combining NVFP4 quantization with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), accelerating rollout phase of RL while reducing memory overhead. Beyond efficiency, our findings show that quantization noise increases policy entropy, enhancing exploration, and enabling the discovery of better strategies during RL. To further optimize exploration, QeRL introduces an Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN) mechanism, which dynamically adjusts noise during training. Experiments demonstrate that QeRL delivers over 1.5 times speedup in the rollout phase. Moreover, this is the first framework to enable RL training of a 32B LLM on a single H100 80GB GPU, while delivering overall speedups for RL training. It also achieves faster reward growth and higher final accuracy than 16-bit LoRA and QLoRA, while matching the performance of full-parameter fine-tuning on mathematical benchmarks such as GSM8K (90.8%) and MATH 500 (77.4%) in the 7B model. These results establish QeRL as an efficient and effective framework for RL training in LLMs.

keyInsights: Quantization noise, traditionally viewed as harmful, can act as a beneficial implicit exploration mechanism in RL by increasing policy entropy, leading to faster reward growth and discovery of better strategies.

Dynamic control over this quantization noise via an adaptive scheduler can balance exploration in early training stages with exploitation in later stages for improved learning stability and performance.

Hardware-aware quantization, exemplified by NVFP4 and optimized kernels, enables simultaneous reduction in memory footprint and acceleration of the inference phase, addressing both memory and speed bottlenecks in RL for LLMs.
originalProblem: The prohibitive computational cost (GPU memory, long rollout durations) of Reinforcement Learning (RL) for enhancing Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning capabilities.
Existing efficiency solutions like QLoRA reduce memory but often slow down the critical rollout (inference) phase, while others like PEFT do not accelerate rollouts.
The conventional view that quantization noise is detrimental to model training, particularly for fine-tuning, limiting its beneficial application in RL.
results: Achieves over 1.5x speedup in the rollout phase and 1.2x–1.5x end-to-end RL training speedup, while reducing GPU memory usage to 25%–30% of 16-bit models.
Successfully enables the RL training of a 32B LLM on a single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU, marking a significant milestone for accessibility in large model training.

Demonstrates superior reasoning performance, achieving 90.8% accuracy on GSM8K for a 7B model (surpassing 16-bit LoRA (88.1%) and QLoRA (85.0%)) and consistently matching or exceeding 16-bit LoRA across various mathematical benchmarks.
solution: Utilizes hardware-accelerated NVFP4 (NVIDIA FP4) quantization combined with Marlin-based kernels for LLM weights to significantly speed up the rollout phase.
Integrates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to reduce the number of trainable parameters and associated memory during fine-tuning.

Introduces Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN) that adds stochastic noise to quantization errors with an exponential decay scheduler, merging this noise into Layer Normalization for zero parameter overhead.
paper_summary.summary: A framework called QeRL significantly improves the efficiency and performance of Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models by employing hardware-accelerated NVFP4 quantization and leveraging quantization noise as a dynamic exploration mechanism. It achieves over 1.5x rollout speedup, enables training of a 32B LLM on a single GPU, and outperforms existing methods in reasoning accuracy on mathematical benchmarks.

title: QeRL: Beyond Efficiency -- Quantization-enhanced Reinforcement Learning for LLMs

abstract:
With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a pivotal methodology for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Unlike traditional pre-training approaches, RL encompasses multiple stages: rollout, reward, and training, which necessitates collaboration among various worker types. However, current RL systems continue to grapple with substantial GPU underutilization, due to two primary factors: (1) The rollout stage dominates the overall RL process due to test-time scaling; (2) Imbalances in rollout lengths (within the same batch) result in GPU bubbles. While prior solutions like asynchronous execution and truncation offer partial relief, they may compromise training accuracy for efficiency.
Our key insight stems from a previously overlooked observation: rollout responses exhibit remarkable similarity across adjacent training epochs. Based on the insight, we introduce RhymeRL, an LLM RL system designed to accelerate RL training with two key innovations. First, to enhance rollout generation, we present HistoSpec, a speculative decoding inference engine that utilizes the similarity of historical rollout token sequences to obtain accurate drafts. Second, to tackle rollout bubbles, we introduce HistoPipe, a two-tier scheduling strategy that leverages the similarity of historical rollout distributions to balance workload among rollout workers. We have evaluated RhymeRL within a real production environment, demonstrating scalability from dozens to thousands of GPUs. Experimental results demonstrate that RhymeRL achieves a 2.6x performance improvement over existing methods, without compromising accuracy or modifying the RL paradigm.

keyInsights: Rollout responses and their length distributions exhibit remarkable similarity across adjacent training epochs for the same prompts, a previously overlooked characteristic of LLM RL.
This historical consistency is primarily due to clipping operations in mainstream RL algorithms (e.g., PPO) that ensure stable model evolution, preventing erratic changes between epochs.

Systematically leveraging this historical information allows for significant acceleration of the rollout process and effective load balancing without compromising algorithmic integrity or training accuracy.
originalProblem: The rollout phase in LLM RL training consumes 84% to 91% of total processing time, driven by autoregressive generation of long reasoning chains and memory-bandwidth-bound token generation.
GPU underutilization occurs due to 'GPU bubbles' where varying response lengths within a batch cause workers to idle, waiting for the longest rollout to complete.
Existing solutions like partial rollouts, batch size adjustments, and asynchronous execution offer limited improvements or compromise accuracy by using highly stale model weights.
results: RhymeRL achieves up to 2.6x end-to-end performance improvement over state-of-the-art LLM RL training systems, with approximately 1.9x to 2.3x average throughput gains for 8K and 16K max response lengths.
It maintains training accuracy, with reward curves closely overlapping those of baseline systems, confirming that its optimizations do not introduce algorithmic degradation.

The system demonstrates scalability from dozens to over a thousand GPUs in a real production environment, with HistoSpec providing up to 1.86x per-step rollout throughput gains and HistoPipe reducing training time per 10 steps by up to 1.68x.
solution: RhymeRL introduces HistoSpec, a history-based speculative decoding mechanism that uses past successful rollouts as drafts to predict and verify multiple tokens in a single forward pass, accelerating generation.

It implements HistoPipe, a two-tier, distribution-aware scheduling strategy that exploits historical rollout length distributions for inter-step workload complementarity and includes migration-based rebalancing for outliers.
The system uses a streaming pipeline architecture with asynchronous weight updates and dedicated history workers on idle CPU resources to manage and index historical rollout information efficiently.
paper_summary.summary: RhymeRL is an LLM reinforcement learning (RL) system that leverages historical rollout information to accelerate training. It achieves up to 2.6x end-to-end performance improvement over state-of-the-art methods while maintaining algorithmic accuracy and demonstrating scalability in production environments.

title: History Rhymes: Accelerating LLM Reinforcement Learning with RhymeRL

abstract: Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) facilitates efficient inference by distilling multi-step diffusion models into few-step variants. Concurrently, Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a vital tool for aligning generative models with human preferences. While both represent critical post-training stages for large-scale diffusion models, existing studies typically treat them as independent, sequential processes, leaving a systematic framework for their unification largely unexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that jointly optimizing these two objectives yields mutual benefits: RL enables more preference-aware and controllable distillation rather than uniformly compressing the full data distribution, while DMD serves as an effective regularizer to mitigate reward hacking during RL training. Building on these insights, we propose DMDR, a unified framework that incorporates Reward-Tilted Distribution Matching optimization alongside two dynamic distillation training strategies in the initial stage, followed by the joint DMD and RL optimization in the second stage. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DMDR achieves state-of-the-art visual quality and prompt adherence among few-step generation methods, even surpassing the performance of its multi-step teacher model.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Concurrently optimizing DMD and RL yields mutual benefits, with RL guiding distillation towards preferred regions and DMD acting as a robust regularizer to prevent reward hacking.
Integrating reward signals into the distillation process from the outset via Reward-Tilted Distribution Matching provides a "warm start" for preference alignment, accelerating training.

Dynamic distillation strategies like Dynamic Distribution Guidance and Dynamic Renoise Sampling effectively mitigate the "cold-start" problem by stabilizing early training and providing reliable optimization signals.
originalProblem: High-resolution text-to-image synthesis using diffusion models is computationally intensive and slow due to the large number of iterative denoising steps required.
Base generative models often produce images that do not consistently align with human aesthetic criteria or specific preferences, necessitating refinement.

Current methods treat step-distillation for efficiency and Reinforcement Learning for preference alignment as separate or sequential processes, which limits their combined potential and can lead to issues like reward hacking or performance ceilings.
results: DMDR's 4-step models consistently outperformed their original multi-step teacher models (e.g., 50 NFE) in overall scores on benchmarks like GenEval and DPG Bench, demonstrating enhanced generative capabilities.
The joint DMD+RL optimization significantly improved test rewards and DPG scores, demonstrating DMD's effectiveness in regularizing RL and preventing reward hacking observed in sequential approaches.
DMDR-distilled models achieved high scores across CLIP, Aesthetic, Pick, and Human Preference metrics, often surpassing other distillation-only and sequential methods on various base models.
solution: A two-stage framework, DMDR, integrates Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) into a unified training pipeline.

Stage 1, Reward-Tilted Distribution Matching (RT-DM) with Dynamic Distillation, softly integrates reward signals early by biasing distillation towards high-reward regions and using dynamic strategies (DynaDG, DynaRS) to stabilize training and address the "cold-start" problem.
Stage 2 employs a joint RL + DMD optimization objective, where DMD acts as a robust regularizer to prevent reward hacking while RL aggressively drives preference alignment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from HKUST, Alibaba Group, CUHK, and other institutions developed DMDR, a two-stage framework that unifies Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for diffusion models. This approach enables few-step generative models to consistently surpass the performance of multi-step teacher models in terms of visual quality and prompt adherence, achieving higher scores on benchmarks like GenEval and DPG Bench while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Distribution Matching Distillation Meets Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Synchronous Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training has emerged as a crucial step for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs) with diverse capabilities. However, many systems designed to accelerate RL post-training still suffer from low resource utilization and limited scalability. We present ROLL Flash, a system that extends ROLL with native support for asynchronous RL post-training. ROLL Flash is built upon two core design principles: fine-grained parallelism and rollout-train decoupling. Guided by these principles, ROLL Flash provides flexible programming interfaces that enable a fully asynchronous training architecture and support efficient rollout mechanisms, including queue scheduling and environment-level asynchronous execution. Through comprehensive theoretical analysis and extensive experiments, we demonstrate that ROLL Flash significantly improves resource utilization and scalability over synchronous RL post-training. ROLL Flash achieves up to 2.24x speedup on RLVR tasks and 2.72x on agentic tasks, using the same GPU budget as synchronous baselines. Furthermore, we implement several popular off-policy algorithms and verify that asynchronous training can achieve performance on par with synchronous training.

keyInsights: Asynchronous execution, when combined with fine-grained parallelism and rollout-train decoupling, can substantially accelerate LLM RL post-training without compromising final model accuracy.
A small 'asynchronous ratio' (e.g., 2) is sufficient to achieve high throughput while effectively balancing learning efficiency and managing off-policy drift.
Dedicated architectural and algorithmic support for agentic tasks can significantly improve training efficiency for environments with high and variable latencies.
originalProblem: Existing RL post-training systems suffer from low resource utilization and limited scalability due to the long-tail distribution of LLM response lengths during rollout.
Synchronous training pipelines create significant GPU idle time by imposing strict synchronization barriers between the rollout and training stages.
Efficiently training LLM agents for complex, multi-turn tasks with high and variable environment latencies poses a significant challenge.
results: ROLL Flash achieves up to 2.24x higher throughput compared to traditional synchronous baselines on 128 GPUs for RLVR tasks with Qwen3-8B-Base.
It delivers an overall 2.72x speedup on ALFWorld and 1.81x on SWE for agentic tasks by combining environment-level asynchronous and redundant rollouts.

The system consistently maintains or slightly improves model performance across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating stability with various off-policy algorithms under asynchronous settings.
solution: ROLL Flash introduces fine-grained parallelism at the sample level and decouples rollout from training, allowing parallel execution through dedicated components like LLMProxy and EnvManager.
It employs queue scheduling with prompt replication to mitigate straggler effects and uses an 'asynchronous ratio' to manage policy staleness and ensure per-sample freshness.

For agentic tasks, it implements environment-level asynchronous rollout and redundant environment rollout to reduce GPU idleness during variable environment interactions and enhance resilience to failures.
paper_summary.summary: ROLL Flash enhances the efficiency and scalability of Reinforcement Learning post-training for Large Language Models by introducing asynchronous execution and fine-grained parallelism. This system achieves up to 2.24x higher throughput for RLVR tasks and a 2.72x speedup for agentic tasks, while maintaining or improving final model performance.

title: Part II: ROLL Flash - Accelerating RLVR and Agentic Training with Asynchrony

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is essential for enhancing the complex reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, existing RL training pipelines are computationally inefficient and resource-intensive, with the rollout phase accounting for over 70% of total training time. Quantized RL training, particularly using FP8 precision, offers a promising approach to mitigating this bottleneck. A commonly adopted strategy applies FP8 precision during rollout while retaining BF16 precision for training. In this work, we present the first comprehensive study of FP8 RL training and demonstrate that the widely used BF16-training + FP8-rollout strategy suffers from severe training instability and catastrophic accuracy collapse under long-horizon rollouts and challenging tasks. Our analysis shows that these failures stem from the off-policy nature of the approach, which introduces substantial numerical mismatch between training and inference. Motivated by these observations, we propose Jet-RL, an FP8 RL training framework that enables robust and stable RL optimization. The key idea is to adopt a unified FP8 precision flow for both training and rollout, thereby minimizing numerical discrepancies and eliminating the need for inefficient inter-step calibration. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of Jet-RL: our method achieves up to 33% speedup in the rollout phase, up to 41% speedup in the training phase, and a 16% end-to-end speedup over BF16 training, while maintaining stable convergence across all settings and incurring negligible accuracy degradation.
title: Jet-RL: Enabling On-Policy FP8 Reinforcement Learning with Unified Training and Rollout Precision Flow

abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is increasingly utilized to enhance the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, effectively scaling these RL methods presents significant challenges, primarily due to the difficulty in maintaining high AI accelerator utilization without generating stale, off-policy data that harms common RL algorithms. This paper introduces PipelineRL, an approach designed to achieve a superior trade-off between hardware efficiency and data on-policyness for LLM training. PipelineRL employs concurrent asynchronous data generation and model training, distinguished by the novel in-flight weight updates. This mechanism allows the LLM generation engine to receive updated model weights with minimal interruption during the generation of token sequences, thereby maximizing both the accelerator utilization and the freshness of training data. Experiments conducted on long-form reasoning tasks using 128 H100 GPUs demonstrate that PipelineRL achieves approximately $\sim 2x$ faster learning compared to conventional RL baselines while maintaining highly on-policy training data. A scalable and modular open-source implementation of PipelineRL is also released as a key contribution.

keyInsights: By continuously updating model weights on actors mid-generation, PipelineRL bypasses the traditional trade-off, significantly boosting both hardware utilization and the 'freshness' of training data.
Retaining a 'stale' KV cache for tokens generated by older weights during in-flight updates has minimal impact on policy divergence, validating an efficient design choice for long sequences.

An asynchronous, pipelined architecture with dynamic weight updates is crucial for scaling RL effectively for LLMs, especially for long-form reasoning tasks where on-policyness and generation speed are paramount.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models (LLMs) faces inefficient scaling, as adding more hardware often yields diminishing returns due to low per-accelerator batch sizes.

A fundamental trade-off exists between maximizing hardware throughput (requiring large batches, leading to off-policy data) and maintaining data on-policyness (requiring frequent updates, leading to low throughput).
Long sequence generation tasks exacerbate these issues, causing extensive synchronous waits, significant accelerator idle time, and increased data staleness if throughput is prioritized.
results: PipelineRL achieved approximately ~2x faster learning speed (time-to-reward) compared to conventional RL baselines configured for high throughput.
It demonstrated ~2x faster sample throughput on a 128 H100 GPU setup by ensuring higher hardware utilization and eliminating idle times.

The method maintained comparable learning effectiveness (sample efficiency) and on-policyness (measured by Effective Sample Size) to less-lagged conventional RL settings, while successfully achieving competitive performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks like MATH500 and AIME2024.
solution: PipelineRL introduces a three-stage asynchronous actor-trainer pipeline (Actor, Preprocessor, Trainer) that runs concurrently to decouple data generation from model training.
The core innovation is 'in-flight weight updates,' allowing generation actors to receive and immediately use updated policy weights while still in the middle of generating a token sequence.
The system utilizes distributed vLLM for actors and DeepSpeed for trainers, connected via Redis, with a modular API for flexible and efficient communication and weight transfer.
paper_summary.summary: PipelineRL accelerates on-policy reinforcement learning for large language models, particularly in long sequence generation tasks, achieving approximately 2x faster learning and throughput compared to conventional methods. This approach, validated on 128 H100 GPUs, maintains comparable sample efficiency and on-policyness, enhancing the scalability of LLM training for complex reasoning tasks.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy Reinforcement Learning for Long Sequence Generation

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a cornerstone in advancing large-scale pre-trained language models (LLMs). Successive generations, including GPT-o series, DeepSeek-R1, Kimi-K1.5, Grok 4, and GLM-4.5, have relied on large-scale RL training to enhance reasoning and coding capabilities. To meet the community's growing RL needs, numerous RL frameworks have been proposed. However, RL training remains computationally expensive, with rollout generation accounting for more than 90% of total runtime. In addition, its efficiency is often constrained by the long-tail distribution of rollout response lengths, where a few lengthy responses stall entire batches, leaving GPUs idle and underutilized. As model and rollout sizes continue to grow, this bottleneck increasingly limits scalability. To address this challenge, we propose Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning (APRIL), which mitigates long-tail inefficiency. In the rollout phase, APRIL over-provisions rollout requests, terminates once the target number of responses is reached, and recycles incomplete responses for continuation in future steps. This strategy ensures that no rollouts are discarded while substantially reducing GPU idle time. Experiments show that APRIL improves rollout throughput by 22.5% on average (at most 44%) across commonly used RL algorithms (GRPO, DAPO, GSPO), accelerates convergence, and achieves 2.1% on average(at most 8%) higher final accuracy across tasks. Moreover, APRIL is both framework and hardware agnostic, already integrated into the slime RL framework, and deployable on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs alike. Taken together, this work unifies system-level and algorithmic considerations in proposing APRIL, with the aim of advancing RL training efficiency and inspiring further optimizations in RL systems. Our codebase is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Introducing a 'mild' form of off-policy data, through the resumption of partial rollouts initiated by prior policies, does not destabilize training; instead, it frequently enhances convergence and final accuracy.
The long-tail problem is predominantly a batch-level issue, as responses generated for a single input instance show significantly less length variability, making instance-level completion viable.
Approximately 40% of tokens in subsequent training steps can originate from these effectively reused partial rollouts, demonstrating substantial resource utilization gains without discarding data.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning (RL) training for Large Language Models (LLMs) is computationally expensive, with rollout generation consuming over 90% of the total runtime.
The 'long-tail distribution' of rollout response lengths leads to significant GPU idle time, as synchronous RL batches wait for the longest responses to complete.
Existing synchronous RL frameworks suffer from this inefficiency despite offering training stability, while asynchronous methods improve utilization but introduce data staleness.
results: APRIL consistently improved rollout throughput by an average of 22.5% across various LLMs (Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B), RL algorithms, and datasets.
The approach led to faster convergence and an average of 2.1% higher final accuracy on the AIME-2024 benchmark, while also preventing 'response length explosion'.
APRIL proved to be framework and hardware agnostic, demonstrating successful deployment and efficiency on both NVIDIA H100 and AMD MI300 GPUs.
solution: APRIL employs over-provisioned rollout generation, launching more requests than strictly needed (N' > N) in each training iteration.
It actively terminates generation once the target number of completed rollouts (N) is reached, storing any unfinished rollouts in a continuation buffer.
In subsequent iterations, APRIL prioritizes resuming these buffered, incomplete rollouts before initiating new ones, ensuring all computational effort is utilized.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed APRIL, a system-level scheduling mechanism that improves the efficiency of reinforcement learning for large language models by actively managing varied rollout response lengths. It achieves a 22.5% average increase in rollout throughput and yields 2.1% higher final accuracy on average.

title: APRIL: Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning to Tame Long-tail Generation

abstract: Existing text-to-image diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, but face significant efficiency challenges when scaled to high resolutions, like 4K image generation. While previous research accelerates diffusion models in various aspects, it seldom handles the inherent redundancy within the latent space. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces DC-Gen, a general framework that accelerates text-to-image diffusion models by leveraging a deeply compressed latent space. Rather than a costly training-from-scratch approach, DC-Gen uses an efficient post-training pipeline to preserve the quality of the base model. A key challenge in this paradigm is the representation gap between the base model's latent space and a deeply compressed latent space, which can lead to instability during direct fine-tuning. To overcome this, DC-Gen first bridges the representation gap with a lightweight embedding alignment training. Once the latent embeddings are aligned, only a small amount of LoRA fine-tuning is needed to unlock the base model's inherent generation quality. We verify DC-Gen's effectiveness on SANA and FLUX.1-Krea. The resulting DC-Gen-SANA and DC-Gen-FLUX models achieve quality comparable to their base models but with a significant speedup. Specifically, DC-Gen-FLUX reduces the latency of 4K image generation by 53x on the NVIDIA H100 GPU. When combined with NVFP4 SVDQuant, DC-Gen-FLUX generates a 4K image in just 3.5 seconds on a single NVIDIA 5090 GPU, achieving a total latency reduction of 138x compared to the base FLUX.1-Krea model. Code: this https URL.

keyInsights: Deeply compressed latent spaces can drastically reduce visual token counts, leading to significant inference speedups for diffusion models without compromising image quality.

A dedicated embedding alignment phase for the patch embedder and output head is crucial for stabilizing the fine-tuning process and preventing catastrophic forgetting when integrating new, deeply compressed autoencoders.
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tuning is an effective and efficient method to adapt large pretrained diffusion models to new latent spaces while preserving their extensive knowledge.

Guidance-distilled diffusion models require a specific corrected flow-matching objective for successful adaptation to deeply compressed latent spaces, ensuring unbiased velocity estimation and quality preservation.
originalProblem: Generating high-resolution images (e.g., 4K) with state-of-the-art text-to-image diffusion models is computationally intensive and slow due to the large number of latent tokens processed.
Training a new, high-quality diffusion model from scratch on a deeply compressed latent space is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming.
Directly replacing an existing autoencoder with a deeply compressed one in a pretrained diffusion model creates a "representation gap" that leads to training instability and catastrophic forgetting.
results: DC-Gen-FLUX achieved a 52.92x reduction in latency and a 56.46x improvement in throughput for 4K image generation on an NVIDIA H100 GPU, effectively enabling native 4K output for models like FLUX.1-Krea.

The entire post-training process for the 12B parameter DC-Gen-FLUX model required only 40 H100 GPU days, representing a 520x reduction in training cost compared to training similar models from scratch.

When combined with NVFP4 SVDQuant (4-bit quantization), DC-Gen-FLUX demonstrated a 138x reduction in end-to-end latency for 4K image generation, highlighting its complementarity with other acceleration techniques.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the embedding alignment stage and LoRA fine-tuning are essential for stable convergence, high-quality image generation, and effective adaptation of diffusion models to deeply compressed latent spaces.
solution: DC-Gen introduces a two-stage post-training pipeline to adapt pretrained diffusion models to deeply compressed latent spaces without training from scratch.

The first stage involves lightweight embedding alignment training for the patch embedder and output head, which maps the new deeply compressed latent features to the diffusion model's internal representations, mitigating the representation gap.

The second stage utilizes LoRA-based end-to-end fine-tuning of the diffusion model, using a standard flow-matching objective or a specially derived corrected objective for guidance-distilled models like FLUX.1-Krea, to preserve knowledge and fully adapt to the new latent space.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA researchers developed DC-Gen, a post-training method that adapts text-to-image diffusion models to deeply compressed latent spaces, thereby accelerating high-resolution image generation. This framework enabled the FLUX.1-Krea model to achieve a 52.92x latency reduction and 56.46x throughput improvement for 4K image generation on an NVIDIA H100 GPU, all while maintaining generation quality and requiring only 40 H100 GPU days for adaptation.

title: DC-Gen: Post-Training Diffusion Acceleration with Deeply Compressed Latent Space

abstract: Diffusion Models have emerged as a leading class of generative models, yet their iterative sampling process remains computationally expensive. Timestep distillation is a promising technique to accelerate generation, but it often requires extensive training and leads to image quality degradation. Furthermore, fine-tuning these distilled models for specific objectives, such as aesthetic appeal or user preference, using Reinforcement Learning (RL) is notoriously unstable and easily falls into reward hacking. In this work, we introduce Flash-DMD, a novel framework that enables fast convergence with distillation and joint RL-based refinement. Specifically, we first propose an efficient timestep-aware distillation strategy that significantly reduces training cost with enhanced realism, outperforming DMD2 with only $2.1\%$ its training cost. Second, we introduce a joint training scheme where the model is fine-tuned with an RL objective while the timestep distillation training continues simultaneously. We demonstrate that the stable, well-defined loss from the ongoing distillation acts as a powerful regularizer, effectively stabilizing the RL training process and preventing policy collapse. Extensive experiments on score-based and flow matching models show that our proposed Flash-DMD not only converges significantly faster but also achieves state-of-the-art generation quality in the few-step sampling regime, outperforming existing methods in visual quality, human preference, and text-image alignment metrics. Our work presents an effective paradigm for training efficient, high-fidelity, and stable generative models. Codes are coming soon.

keyInsights: Decoupling distillation objectives based on the current denoising timestep (high versus low noise) leads to faster convergence and enhanced perceptual quality by addressing conflicting gradients.
Leveraging a powerful pre-trained vision model (SAM) within the discriminator effectively enforces realism and diversity, preventing the distilled model from falling into mode-seeking behaviors.

Jointly training reinforcement learning with distillation loss, combined with a timestep-aware reward model and strategic high-noise sampling, stabilizes RL optimization and effectively prevents reward hacking artifacts.
originalProblem: Diffusion models are computationally expensive and slow, requiring numerous iterative steps for high-quality image generation.
Existing diffusion distillation methods incur extensive training costs, exhibit unstable training dynamics, and often suffer from mode-seeking behavior.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning for few-step distilled models is prone to "reward hacking," leading to undesirable visual artifacts like "oil painting" effects.
results: Flash-DMD achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced training costs, requiring as little as 2.1% of previous distillation method's (DMD2) computational resources for comparable human preference scores.

The method consistently yields higher human preference scores (e.g., ImageReward, PickScore, HPSv2, MPS) and improved perceptual realism compared to teacher models and other distilled baselines, effectively mitigating reward hacking artifacts.
The framework demonstrates strong generalizability, achieving superior results when applied to both score-based (SDXL) and flow matching (SD3-Medium) generative model architectures.
solution: A two-stage framework employs a timestep-aware distillation strategy, decoupling loss functions based on noise levels: Distribution Matching for high-noise steps and an adversarial Pixel-GAN loss for low-noise steps.
A Pixel-GAN discriminator, built upon a frozen Segment Anything Model (SAM) vision encoder, is used to impose stringent realism constraints and alleviate mode-seeking from early training.

Joint reinforcement learning integrates a timestep-aware Latent Reward Model (LRM) and optimizes preferences by sampling at high-noise timesteps, with the distillation loss acting as a regularizer to prevent reward hacking.
paper_summary.summary: Flash-DMD, a collaboration from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Tencent, and other institutions, introduces an efficient framework for high-fidelity, few-step image generation by integrating a timestep-aware distillation strategy with joint reinforcement learning. The method significantly reduces training costs while producing images with improved perceptual quality and successfully prevents reward hacking artifacts during preference alignment.

title: Flash-DMD: Towards High-Fidelity Few-Step Image Generation with Efficient Distillation and Joint Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training is crucial for aligning generative models with human preferences, but its prohibitive computational cost remains a major barrier to widespread adoption. We introduce \textbf{TreeGRPO}, a novel RL framework that dramatically improves training efficiency by recasting the denoising process as a search tree. From shared initial noise samples, TreeGRPO strategically branches to generate multiple candidate trajectories while efficiently reusing their common prefixes. This tree-structured approach delivers three key advantages: (1) \emph{High sample efficiency}, achieving better performance under same training samples (2) \emph{Fine-grained credit assignment} via reward backpropagation that computes step-specific advantages, overcoming the uniform credit assignment limitation of trajectory-based methods, and (3) \emph{Amortized computation} where multi-child branching enables multiple policy updates per forward pass. Extensive experiments on both diffusion and flow-based models demonstrate that TreeGRPO achieves \textbf{2.4$\times$ faster training} while establishing a superior Pareto frontier in the efficiency-reward trade-off space. Our method consistently outperforms GRPO baselines across multiple benchmarks and reward models, providing a scalable and effective pathway for RL-based visual generative model alignment. The project website is available at this http URL.

keyInsights: The tree-structured advantage estimation provides principled variance reduction, leading to more stable and efficient gradient estimates during training.
Propagating rewards from leaves to the root enables fine-grained, step-specific credit assignment, allowing the policy to learn more effectively from individual denoising actions.
Amortized computation from multi-child branching allows for multiple policy updates per forward pass, significantly improving training speed.
originalProblem: Existing reinforcement learning (RL) post-training methods for visual generative models incur prohibitive computational costs, hindering scalability.
Current GRPO-based approaches suffer from poor sample efficiency, requiring complete denoising trajectories for each policy update.
These methods utilize coarse credit assignment, uniformly applying a single terminal reward to all denoising steps without distinguishing individual action contributions.
results: TreeGRPO achieves approximately 2.4 times faster training convergence compared to leading baselines such as DanceGRPO, establishing a superior efficiency-reward trade-off.
The method consistently obtains higher alignment performance, achieving the best HPS-v2.1 score (0.3735) and Aesthetic score (6.5094) in single-reward training.

Ablation studies reveal that a branching factor of k=3 and depth of d=3 offers the optimal balance of performance and efficiency, while strategic random window sampling further enhances specific alignment goals.
solution: Recasts the fixed-horizon denoising process as a Markov Decision Process and converts deterministic ODEs into equivalent Stochastic Differential Equations (SDEs) for RL compatibility.
Employs a tree-structured sampler with random window branching, allowing the reuse of common prefixes while generating diverse candidate trajectories within SDE windows.
Calculates fine-grained, step-specific advantages by backpropagating leaf-level rewards through the tree using a probability-weighted aggregation mechanism.
Updates the policy using a GRPO objective, applying a PPO-style clipped surrogate loss on these derived per-edge advantages.
paper_summary.summary: TreeGRPO introduces a reinforcement learning framework that reinterprets diffusion model denoising as a sparse search tree, enabling both sample efficiency and precise credit assignment for post-training. This method achieves 2.4 times faster training convergence and enhances alignment quality with human preferences compared to prior approaches.

title: TreeGRPO: Tree-Advantage GRPO for Online RL Post-Training of Diffusion Models

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as an effective post-training paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language model (MLLM). However, current RL pipelines often suffer from training inefficiencies caused by two underexplored issues: Advantage Collapsing, where most advantages in a batch concentrate near zero, and Rollout Silencing, where the proportion of rollouts contributing non-zero gradients diminishes over time. These issues lead to suboptimal gradient updates and hinder long-term learning efficiency. To address these issues, we propose Shuffle-R1, a simple yet principled framework that improves RL fine-tuning efficiency by dynamically restructuring trajectory sampling and batch composition. It introduces (1) Pairwise Trajectory Sampling, which selects high-contrast trajectories with large advantages to improve gradient signal quality, and (2) Advantage-based Trajectory Shuffle, which increases exposure of valuable rollouts through informed batch reshuffling. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks show that our framework consistently outperforms strong RL baselines with minimal overhead. These results highlight the importance of data-centric adaptations for more efficient RL training in MLLM.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: Shuffle-R1: Efficient RL framework for Multimodal Large Language Models via Data-centric Dynamic Shuffle

abstract: Reinforcement learning(RL) post-training has become essential for aligning large language models (LLMs), yet its efficiency is increasingly constrained by the rollout phase, where long trajectories are generated token by token. We identify a major bottleneck:the long-tail distribution of rollout lengths, where a small fraction of long generations dominates wall clock time and a complementary opportunity; the availability of historical rollouts that reveal stable prompt level patterns across training epochs. Motivated by these observations, we propose DAS, a Distribution Aware Speculative decoding framework that accelerates RL rollouts without altering model outputs. DAS integrates two key ideas: an adaptive, nonparametric drafter built from recent rollouts using an incrementally maintained suffix tree, and a length aware speculation policy that allocates more aggressive draft budgets to long trajectories that dominate makespan. This design exploits rollout history to sustain acceptance while balancing base and token level costs during decoding. Experiments on math and code reasoning tasks show that DAS reduces rollout time up to 50% while preserving identical training curves, demonstrating that distribution-aware speculative decoding can significantly accelerate RL post training without compromising learning quality.

keyInsights: The rollout phase, rather than model weight updates, is now the dominant bottleneck in modern LLM RL training, driven by increasing generation lengths and sample sizes.
Policy drift in continuously updating RL models renders static neural drafters for speculative decoding quickly obsolete, necessitating adaptive, model-free approaches.
The long-tail distribution of rollout lengths significantly impacts overall training makespan, requiring a distribution-aware speculation strategy to prioritize resource allocation.
originalProblem: The rollout phase, where LLMs generate trajectories to collect experience, constitutes over 70% of total LLM RL training time, becoming the primary bottleneck.
Long-tail sequences within rollout batches create 'stragglers' that dictate overall training step makespan and lead to poor GPU utilization.
Existing speculative decoding techniques designed for static LLM serving systems are ineffective in RL training due to continuous policy drift and specific latency requirements.
results: Achieved up to a 50% reduction in total rollout time per training step for mathematical reasoning tasks and approximately 25% for code generation RL workloads.
Demonstrated identical training curves and reward scores compared to non-speculative baselines, confirming lossless acceleration without compromising learning quality.
The adaptive nonparametric drafter showed continuously improving acceptance rates, empirically outperforming static drafters and suffix arrays in efficiency and update speed.
solution: Introduces an adaptive, nonparametric drafter based on an incrementally updated suffix tree, constructed from a sliding window of recent rollouts to adapt to policy drift.
Proposes a length-aware speculation policy that dynamically allocates higher speculative token budgets to longer, high-difficulty prompts, specifically targeting the 'long-tail' latency bottleneck.
Integrates these components into a comprehensive systems-ML codesign framework, built on existing RL training (VeRL) and LLM inference (vLLM) engines, to ensure lossless acceleration.
paper_summary.summary: A distribution-aware speculative decoding approach (DAS) accelerates the rollout phase of Large Language Model (LLM) reinforcement learning (RL) training, achieving up to a 50% reduction in wall-clock time while maintaining identical learning curves and reward scores. The method effectively addresses the long-tail latency problem and policy drift inherent in RL workflows.

title: Beat the long tail: Distribution-Aware Speculative Decoding for RL Training

abstract: Diffusion model alignment aims to bridge the gap between generated outputs and human preferences by enhancing both semantic consistency with textual prompts and overall visual quality. Existing alignment methods face a challenging trade-off: test-time approaches enable input-specific adaptability but introduce significant computational overhead and tend to under-optimize, while fine-tuning approaches risk reward over-optimization and loss of generation diversity. To bridge this gap, we propose HyperAlign, a framework that trains a hypernetwork for efficient and effective test-time alignment. Instead of modifying latent states directly, HyperAlign dynamically generates input-and-state-conditioned low-rank adaptation weights to modulate the denoising trajectory toward target rewards. We introduce multiple HyperAlign variants of varying granularity to balance alignment quality and computational efficiency. The hypernetwork is optimized with a reward objective regularized by preference data to mitigate reward hacking. We evaluate HyperAlign across multiple generative paradigms, including Stable Diffusion and FLUX, where it significantly outperforms existing alignment methods in semantic consistency and visual quality.

organization_info.name: University of New South Wales (UNSW Sydney)

title: HyperAlign: Hypernetwork for Efficient Test-Time Alignment of Diffusion Models

abstract: Aligning generative diffusion models with human preferences via reinforcement learning (RL) is critical yet challenging. Most existing algorithms are often vulnerable to reward hacking, such as quality degradation, over-stylization, or reduced diversity. Our analysis demonstrates that this can be attributed to the inherent limitations of their regularization, which provides unreliable penalties. We introduce Data-regularized Diffusion Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), a novel framework that uses the forward KL divergence to anchor the policy to an off-policy data distribution. Theoretically, DDRL enables robust, unbiased integration of RL with standard diffusion training. Empirically, this translates into a simple yet effective algorithm that combines reward maximization with diffusion loss minimization. With over a million GPU hours of experiments and ten thousand double-blind human evaluations, we demonstrate on high-resolution video generation tasks that DDRL significantly improves rewards while alleviating the reward hacking seen in baselines, achieving the highest human preference and establishing a robust and scalable paradigm for diffusion post-training.

keyInsights: Regularizing the policy using forward KL divergence against an off-policy data distribution directly corresponds to the standard diffusion training loss, offering a principled and robust regularization signal.
Anchoring the policy to the actual data manifold prevents policy drift into out-of-distribution regions, effectively mitigating reward hacking without compromising generation quality or realism.
DDRL allows for a unified post-training pipeline, potentially combining supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning into a single, more data-efficient stage.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training for diffusion models frequently suffers from reward hacking, where models generate high-reward but human-unpreferred outputs due to imperfections in the reward function.

Existing RL regularization techniques for diffusion models, primarily using reverse KL divergence with on-policy samples, are unreliable and allow the generative policy to drift into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions.
This OOD drift leads to quality degradation, over-stylization, or reduced diversity in generated content, undermining the goal of aligning models with human preferences at scale.
results: DDRL consistently achieved the highest human preference across diverse tasks (T2V/I2V) and model scales (Cosmos2.5-2B/14B), demonstrating superiority over baselines like DanceGRPO and FlowGRPO.

The method demonstrated Pareto improvement in generative properties, enhancing multiple attributes (e.g., text alignment, visual quality, motion quality) simultaneously without degradation, unlike baselines that show trade-offs indicative of hacking.

DDRL proved robust to extended training without exhibiting hacking behaviors and was shown to be computationally efficient, effectively replacing the multi-stage post-training pipeline with a single 'Pretrained+DDRL' approach that maintained performance with higher data efficiency.
solution: DDRL introduces a novel regularization strategy that anchors the generative policy to an off-policy data distribution using forward KL divergence, which is theoretically equivalent to the standard diffusion training objective.

The approach combines reward maximization with standard diffusion loss minimization into a single objective function, eliminating the need for a separate reference model or an 'old policy' during training.

The practical algorithm integrates both components through gradient descent, sampling from the current policy for rewards and from an off-policy data sampler for diffusion loss, making it computationally efficient.
paper_summary.summary: Data-regularized Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), developed by researchers from Stanford University and NVIDIA, mitigates reward hacking in large-scale diffusion models by introducing an off-policy data regularization term. This method consistently achieves higher human preference scores across video and image generation tasks, ensuring genuinely aligned and high-quality outputs unlike prior reinforcement learning approaches.

title: Data-regularized Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Models at Scale

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) is increasingly bottlenecked by rollout (generation), where long output sequence lengths make attention and KV-cache memory dominate end-to-end step time. FP8 offers an attractive lever for accelerating RL by reducing compute cost and memory traffic during rollout, but applying FP8 in RL introduces unique engineering and algorithmic challenges: policy weights change every step (requiring repeated quantization and weight synchronization into the inference engine) and low-precision rollouts can deviate from the higher-precision policy assumed by the trainer, causing train-inference mismatch and potential instability. This report presents a practical FP8 rollout stack for LLM RL, implemented in the veRL ecosystem with support for common training backends (e.g., FSDP/Megatron-LM) and inference engines (e.g., vLLM/SGLang). We (i) enable FP8 W8A8 linear-layer rollout using blockwise FP8 quantization, (ii) extend FP8 to KV-cache to remove long-context memory bottlenecks via per-step QKV scale recalibration, and (iii) mitigate mismatch using importance-sampling-based rollout correction (token-level TIS/MIS variants). Across dense and MoE models, these techniques deliver up to 44% rollout throughput gains while preserving learning behavior comparable to BF16 baselines.

keyInsights: FP8 quantization can significantly accelerate LLM RL rollouts without degrading learning quality when combined with effective mismatch mitigation techniques like TIS.
Optimizing both compute-bound (linear layers) and memory-bound (KV-cache) components with FP8 provides complementary and substantial performance gains, especially for long-context generation.
An end-to-end FP8 strategy across both rollout and training not only reduces overall computation time but also helps align numerical precision, leading to a smaller train-inference mismatch.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models (LLM RL), especially for alignment, is computationally intensive.
The "rollout" phase, where the policy generates responses, constitutes a significant bottleneck, consuming up to 80% of total iteration time.
Applying low-precision FP8 quantization to dynamic LLM RL rollouts is challenging due to the need for continuous weight synchronization and managing train-inference mismatch.
results: FP8-RL achieved approximately 10-20% speedup for dense models and 30-50% for Mixture-of-Experts models during rollouts compared to BF16 baselines.
Combined FP8 linear W8A8 and KV-cache quantization yielded a cumulative speedup of 44% for dense models (e.g., Qwen3-8B), particularly benefiting memory-bound long-context generation.

Training with FP8 rollouts, stabilized by token-level Truncated Importance Sampling, maintained learning behavior and final accuracy comparable to full-precision baselines across various metrics and models.
solution: FP8-RL introduces a comprehensive system within the `veRL` framework, supporting blockwise W8A8 quantization for linear layers and FP8 for the KV-cache during rollouts.
It implements a dynamic weight synchronization pipeline for efficient FP8 re-quantization and loading of updated weights into the inference engine at each RL step.
Token-level Truncated Importance Sampling (TIS) is employed to correct for the distribution shift between the FP8 rollout policy and the higher-precision training policy.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA researchers developed FP8-RL, a comprehensive low-precision stack for Large Language Model Reinforcement Learning rollouts, achieving up to 44% speedup for dense models and 50% for Mixture-of-Experts models while maintaining learning performance comparable to full-precision baselines. The system incorporates FP8 quantization for linear layers and KV-cache, alongside token-level Truncated Importance Sampling to mitigate training-inference mismatch.

title: FP8-RL: A Practical and Stable Low-Precision Stack for LLM Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard technique for post-training diffusion-based image synthesis models, as it enables learning from reward signals to explicitly improve desirable aspects such as image quality and prompt alignment. In this paper, we propose an online RL variant that reduces the variance in the model updates by sampling paired trajectories and pulling the flow velocity in the direction of the more favorable image. Unlike existing methods that treat each sampling step as a separate policy action, we consider the entire sampling process as a single action. We experiment with both high-quality vision language models and off-the-shelf quality metrics for rewards, and evaluate the outputs using a broad set of metrics. Our method converges faster and yields higher output quality and prompt alignment than previous approaches.

keyInsights: Considering the entire image generation process as a single action, rather than individual denoising steps, dramatically improves the signal-to-noise ratio of RL updates, leading to faster and more stable optimization.

An approximate gradient signal derived from finite differences (∆R∆x) can effectively optimize even non-differentiable reward functions without direct gradient access, mitigating issues like reward hacking and artifact generation.

An adapted stochastic sampler that maintains correct noise levels and provides consistent time conditioning is crucial for generating coherent, varied trajectories essential for finite difference optimization.
originalProblem: Current Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods for post-training diffusion models, often based on Markov Decision Processes, suffer from high variance and reward-neutral noise in flow updates.
This noise leads to slow learning progress and uncontrolled drift in image attributes not covered by the reward function, causing unintended stylistic changes.
Extended training with existing methods can accumulate detrimental side effects, such as the introduction of visual artifacts and reward hacking.
results: FDFO achieved significantly faster convergence (up to 19 times faster in baseline configuration) and higher reward levels across PickScore, VLM-based alignment, and combined rewards compared to Flow-GRPO.
The method produced visually superior images with better prompt alignment and reduced instances of undesirable stylistic drift and visual artifacts during extended training.

Ablation studies confirmed that the finite difference optimization mechanism, the adapted stochastic sampler, and the use of shared initial noise were critical for FDFO's superior performance and stability.
solution: FDFO treats the entire image generation process, from initial noise to final image, as a single action, moving away from per-step policy updates.
It generates paired image trajectories from a shared initial noise, introducing a small, controlled amount of stochasticity to create two slightly varied outputs.

A training signal is derived from the product of the reward difference (∆R) and the image difference (∆x) between the paired outputs (∆R∆x), effectively providing an approximate gradient to steer flow velocities towards higher rewards.
paper_summary.summary: Finite Difference Flow Optimization (FDFO), a method for RL post-training of text-to-image diffusion models, achieves significantly faster convergence and higher reward levels by deriving a stable training signal. FDFO reduces artifact generation and style drift compared to previous techniques, producing demonstrably higher quality and better aligned images.

title: Finite Difference Flow Optimization for RL Post-Training of Text-to-Image Models

abstract: Recent advances in diffusion models have revolutionized text-guided image editing, yet existing editing methods face critical challenges in hyperparameter identification. To get the reasonable editing performance, these methods often require the user to brute-force tune multiple interdependent hyperparameters, such as inversion timesteps and attention modification. This process incurs high computational costs due to the huge hyperparameter search space. We consider searching optimal editing's hyperparameters as a sequential decision-making task within the diffusion denoising process. Specifically, we propose a reinforcement learning framework, which establishes a Markov Decision Process that dynamically adjusts hyperparameters across denoising steps, integrating editing objectives into a reward function. The method achieves time efficiency through proximal policy optimization while maintaining optimal hyperparameter configurations. Experiments demonstrate significant reduction in search time and computational overhead compared to existing brute-force approaches, advancing the practical deployment of a diffusion-based image editing framework in the real world. Codes can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning can effectively transform the computational complexity of hyperparameter tuning in diffusion models from an exponential search to a linear time problem (O(T)).

A two-phase RL training strategy, starting with policy initialization from prior distributions, is crucial for stable and effective learning within the large state space of hyperparameter optimization.

Dynamically adapting hyperparameters at each denoising step, guided by a balanced reward function, enables superior image editing quality by simultaneously optimizing prompt alignment and background preservation.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion-based image editing methods rely on manual hyperparameter tuning, which is computationally expensive with an exponential complexity of O(T * N^K) and impractical for real-world deployment.
Optimal hyperparameter configurations are highly sensitive to specific input images and editing prompts, necessitating extensive trial-and-error for each individual task.

Achieving a delicate balance between precise alignment with the editing prompt and minimal alteration of the original image content (background preservation) is challenging with fixed or manually tuned parameters.
results: AutoEdit consistently improved quantitative metrics for background preservation (e.g., PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) and prompt alignment (CLIP score, LLM score) across various diffusion-based editing methods and base models.
The method introduced negligible computational overhead, increasing inference time and model parameters by only approximately 0.6% compared to the original U-Net.
Qualitative results demonstrated more natural and coherent edited images, with a user study showing an 82.78% preference for AutoEdit's outputs over baselines.
solution: Formulates hyperparameter identification as a reinforcement learning (RL) task, treating the diffusion denoising process as a Markov Decision Process to dynamically adjust hyperparameters at each step.
Employs a two-phase RL training strategy: initializing the policy by pretraining with known hyperparameter prior distributions, then optimizing it via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).
Designs a comprehensive reward function that quantifies both background preservation (negative MSE) and prompt alignment (CLIP score for global edits, LVLM-based evaluation for small region edits).
paper_summary.summary: AutoEdit introduces a reinforcement learning framework to automate hyperparameter tuning in diffusion-based image editing, transforming the computational search complexity from exponential to linear. This method consistently enhances editing quality, leading to more natural images with improved background preservation and prompt alignment, and achieved an 82.78% preference in user studies compared to baselines.

title: AutoEdit: Automatic Hyperparameter Tuning for Image Editing

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has improved guided image generation with diffusion models by directly optimizing rewards that capture image quality, aesthetics, and instruction following capabilities. However, the resulting generative policies inherit the same iterative sampling process of diffusion models that causes slow generation. To overcome this limitation, consistency models proposed learning a new class of generative models that directly map noise to data, resulting in a model that can generate an image in as few as one sampling iteration. In this work, to optimize text-to-image generative models for task specific rewards and enable fast training and inference, we propose a framework for fine-tuning consistency models via RL. Our framework, called Reinforcement Learning for Consistency Model (RLCM), frames the iterative inference process of a consistency model as an RL procedure. Comparing to RL finetuned diffusion models, RLCM trains significantly faster, improves the quality of the generation measured under the reward objectives, and speeds up the inference procedure by generating high quality images with as few as two inference steps. Experimentally, we show that RLCM can adapt text-to-image consistency models to objectives that are challenging to express with prompting, such as image compressibility, and those derived from human feedback, such as aesthetic quality. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: RL for Consistency Models: Faster Reward Guided Text-to-Image Generation

abstract: Recent advancements adopt online reinforcement learning (RL) from LLMs to text-to-image rectified flow diffusion models for reward alignment. The use of group-level rewards successfully aligns the model with the targeted reward. However, it faces challenges including low efficiency, dependency on stochastic samplers, and reward hacking. The problem is that rectified flow models are fundamentally different from LLMs: 1) For efficiency, online image sampling takes much more time and dominates the time of training. 2) For stochasticity, rectified flow is deterministic once the initial noise is fixed. Aiming at these problems and inspired by the effects of group-level rewards from LLMs, we design Group-level Direct Reward Optimization (GDRO). GDRO is a new post-training paradigm for group-level reward alignment that combines the characteristics of rectified flow models. Through rigorous theoretical analysis, we point out that GDRO supports full offline training that saves the large time cost for image rollout sampling. Also, it is diffusion-sampler-independent, which eliminates the need for the ODE-to-SDE approximation to obtain stochasticity. We also empirically study the reward hacking trap that may mislead the evaluation, and involve this factor in the evaluation using a corrected score that not only considers the original evaluation reward but also the trend of reward hacking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GDRO effectively and efficiently improves the reward score of the diffusion model through group-wise offline optimization across the OCR and GenEval tasks, while demonstrating strong stability and robustness in mitigating reward hacking.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: An implicit reward function, derived from predicted noise/velocity, enables efficient and sampler-independent reward optimization for diffusion models, crucial for rectified flow architectures.
Optimizing for group-level preferences, theoretically grounded in the Plackett-Luce model, offers a stable alternative to pairwise preferences, effectively generalizing DPO for multiple candidates.

"Reward hacking" is a significant issue in T2I alignment, necessitating a "corrected score" that integrates general image quality metrics (like coherence and style) with task-specific rewards for objective evaluation.
originalProblem: Online reinforcement learning for text-to-image diffusion models is computationally expensive, requiring extensive image rollouts for each optimization step.

Deterministic rectified flow diffusion models are incompatible with traditional policy gradient RL methods that often necessitate stochastic samplers, leading to quality degradation when approximations are used.
Generative models frequently suffer from "reward hacking," optimizing for numerical reward scores at the expense of overall image quality, coherence, or prompt alignment.
results: GDRO achieves strong performance on OCR and GenEval tasks, matching or exceeding baselines like Flow-GRPO in raw scores, but with significantly improved efficiency (e.g., 2x to 3.7x fewer GPU hours).

The proposed "corrected score" and user studies demonstrate GDRO's superior robustness against reward hacking, preserving image quality, coherence, and prompt alignment where baseline methods often degrade.

Ablation studies reveal the importance of group size (optimal around k=6), and the "beta" and "tau" parameters for ensuring training stability and achieving optimal balance between reward maximization and quality.
solution: GDRO proposes an offline, sampler-independent framework that uses an implicit reward function, approximated from the diffusion model's predicted noise or velocity at any timestep, bypassing full image generation.

It applies a group-level direct reward optimization strategy, converting explicit rewards for a group of images into target probability distributions for ranking, and optimizing a cross-entropy loss against the model's implicit scores.
A reconstruction loss regularization term is integrated to stabilize the quality of top-ranked samples, addressing potential quality degradation even when overall ranking improves.
paper_summary.summary: The GDRO framework introduces an offline, sampler-independent group-level direct reward optimization for text-to-image diffusion models, significantly reducing computational cost while mitigating reward hacking. This approach enables more efficient and robust alignment of generative models with desired human preferences, yielding higher quality and prompt-aligned images.

title: GDRO: Group-level Reward Post-training Suitable for Diffusion Models

abstract: Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as promising alternatives to Auto-Regressive (AR) models. While recent efforts have validated their pre-training potential and accelerated inference speeds, the post-training landscape for dLLMs remains underdeveloped. Existing methods suffer from computational inefficiency and objective mismatches between training and inference, severely limiting performance on complex reasoning tasks such as mathematics. To address this, we introduce DiRL, an efficient post-training framework that tightly integrates FlexAttention-accelerated blockwise training with LMDeploy-optimized inference. This architecture enables a streamlined online model update loop, facilitating efficient two-stage post-training (Supervised Fine-Tuning followed by Reinforcement Learning). Building on this framework, we propose DiPO, the first unbiased Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) implementation tailored for dLLMs. We validate our approach by training DiRL-8B-Instruct on high-quality math data. Our model achieves state-of-the-art math performance among dLLMs and surpasses comparable models in the Qwen2.5 series on several benchmarks.

keyInsights: Engineering optimizations, particularly FlexAttention and LMDeploy's online updates, are critical for scalable dLLM post-training, achieving a 2.55x throughput improvement.
DiRL demonstrates that dLLMs, with proper and efficient post-training, can achieve and surpass the reasoning capabilities of larger Auto-Regressive models on complex tasks.
DiPO successfully adapts Group Relative Policy Optimization for dLLMs, providing the first unbiased implementation that resolves logit computation challenges and enables effective policy alignment.
originalProblem: Post-training for Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs), especially Reinforcement Learning (RL), is computationally inefficient and resource-intensive.
Existing dLLM training methods suffer from objective mismatches and biased logit computation, leading to suboptimal performance in complex reasoning tasks.
The lack of robust and unbiased RL algorithms for dLLMs makes it challenging to adapt advanced techniques like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
results: DiRL-8B-Instruct achieves a 2.55x overall throughput improvement per RL training step compared to prior dLLM RL methods like TraceRL.
DiRL-8B-Instruct sets a new state-of-the-art for dLLMs in mathematical reasoning, with an average score of 54.0% across five challenging math benchmarks.
The 8-billion parameter DiRL-8B-Instruct outperforms both Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and, in several key instances, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct on mathematical reasoning tasks.
solution: The DiRL framework employs a two-stage post-training approach: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL) using the DiPO algorithm.
Integrates engineering optimizations such as PyTorch's FlexAttention for efficient handling of irregular attention masks in blockwise dLLMs, drastically reducing training latency.
Achieves tight integration of training and inference by deploying the dLLM in an API server via LMDeploy, enabling online parameter updates and eliminating I/O bottlenecks.
paper_summary.summary: DiRL introduces an efficient post-training framework for Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs), leveraging engineering optimizations and an unbiased Group Relative Policy Optimization (DiPO) algorithm. This framework enables dLLMs to achieve state-of-the-art performance in mathematical reasoning, often outperforming larger auto-regressive models.

title: DiRL: An Efficient Post-Training Framework for Diffusion Language Models

abstract: Recent progress in flow-based generative models and reinforcement learning (RL) has improved text-image alignment and visual quality. However, current RL training for flow models still has two main problems: (i) GRPO-style fixed per-prompt group sizes ignore variation in sampling importance across prompts, which leads to inefficient sampling and slower training; and (ii) trajectory-level advantages are reused as per-step estimates, which biases credit assignment along the flow. We propose SuperFlow, an RL training framework for flow-based models that adjusts group sizes with variance-aware sampling and computes step-level advantages in a way that is consistent with continuous-time flow dynamics. Empirically, SuperFlow reaches promising performance while using only 5.4% to 56.3% of the original training steps and reduces training time by 5.2% to 16.7% without any architectural changes. On standard text-to-image (T2I) tasks, including text rendering, compositional image generation, and human preference alignment, SuperFlow improves over SD3.5-M by 4.6% to 47.2%, and over Flow-GRPO by 1.7% to 16.0%.

keyInsights: Dynamically allocating sampling resources based on prompt-level reward uncertainty substantially improves training efficiency and prevents stability issues observed in fixed-group or single-rollout methods.

Modulating advantage estimates at each denoising step by the inherent stochasticity (diffusion coefficient) of the continuous-time process provides a more granular and accurate mechanism for credit assignment.

Combining adaptive, variance-aware sampling with refined step-level credit assignment leads to robust and efficient policy optimization for continuous-time generative models without requiring architectural modifications.
originalProblem: Existing GRPO-style reinforcement learning (RL) methods for generative models suffer from sampling inefficiency due to fixed-size per-prompt sampling groups, overlooking heterogeneous learning potential.
